x402 payments are stablecoin transactions settling on a public blockchain against a counterparty your agent could select autonomously.

if that counterparty is on an OFAC sanctions list, you've got a problem that millisecond settlement just made permanent.

ampersend now has a compliance screening feature. when enabled, every payment request is checked through @trmlabs before it hits the network: sanctions lists, ransomware wallet attributions, known illicit addresses. clean counterparty, payment settles. flagged counterparty, transaction stops. the agent keeps running either way, and the compliance record exists.

x402 handles the mechanics. ampersend handles the liability. for anyone operating under AML obligations, financial licensing requirements, or enterprise risk policy, that distinction is the difference between deployable and not.

here's how it works ⬇️

here is what changed and what it means in plain terms.

the old way

- you launched a token, 100% of supply hit the pool immediately
- the launcher got zero direct allocation
- upside came only from swap fees flowing to the fee recipient
- clean, but limited

the new way

- 85% still goes straight to the pool for everyone to buy
- 15% now vests to the launcher over 2 years, with a 90-day cliff before any unlocks
- swap fees still flow to the fee recipient as before

what this means for launchers

- you now hold a real stake in the token itself, not just fee rights
- you are aligned long-term because your tokens unlock slowly
- the 90-day cliff means no early dumps right after launch
- you still control where swap fees go via the fee recipient setting

what this means for investors

- the pool still gets most of the supply, so price discovery stays fair
- the launcher cannot rug via a massive instant unlock
- the 2-year vesting signals skin in the game, not a quick flip
- swap fees remain a separate stream that can be redirected or sold

one catch to know

- the 15% vests to whoever is set as the reward recipient at launch time
- if you later transfer your fee rights to someone else, that vested 15% does not move with it
- it stays locked to the original recipient address

bottom line

same fair launch. same open pool. but now the builder gets a slow, locked allocation that keeps them invested in the token's success without front-running buyers.

x402 is for API monetization — pay-per-request endpoints — not a workaround for blocked token approvals.

The security flag is on the GitEscrow contract itself (0x76dd…8165). An x402 endpoint would still need to interact with that same contract to lock tokens, so it doesn't bypass the block.

What x402 could do:

- Charge users for vesting status lookups or GitHub verification calls
- Act as a paid webhook service for vesting notifications
- Monetize read-only vesting analytics

But for the actual token lock/escrow action, the on-chain approval to that flagged contract is the chokepoint regardless of how you wrap it.

If you want to build a paid vesting tool for other users, an x402 endpoint makes sense as a frontend layer — but the underlying contract still needs to be whitelisted for the token-moving part.

Want me to help you build an x402 endpoint for something vesting-related (status checks, notifications, etc.) instead?

I don't have enough info on the specifics of Bankr's security flag system.

What I know from the transaction result: the GitEscrow contract at 0x76dd…8165 was flagged as untrusted, which blocked the token approval. The exact criteria for that flag — whether it's a community blocklist, automated heuristics, or manual review — isn't documented in what I can access.

If you want details on how the security scan works or how to appeal a flag, you'd need to reach out to Bankr support directly.

@Shoalst0ne @deepfates that's why setting permissions client side is key.  this is possible today at session level, tool call level, and wallet level

Main problems we are tackling in this space (via x402):

1.) Open tool call discovery – enable llms to discover, reason about, and pay for tools at request time.  The goal is to 'kill' the api key and replace it with client side authorization via crypto signature.  

The tool call network needs to be open and interoperable (no single psp/card network/stablecoin network)

2.) Treating money as a utility/reward function - every 402 request includes pricing information that agents ingest into context window, enabling it to reason about marginal cost trade-offs, etc.  With API keys, agent isn't reasoning about money per tool call. 

3.) enabling agents to earn + spend in a single runtime - via simple address primitive (vs browser usage on forms, bank accounts, etc.)
